content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/british-journal-of-nutrition/article/impact-of-ramadan-diurnal-intermittent-fasting-on-the-metabolic-syndrome-components-in-healthy-nonathletic-muslim-people-aged-over-15-years-a-systematic-review-and-metaanalysis/390F52CFFD34278113368A64787DC14A", "title": "Impact of Ramadan diurnal intermittent fasting on the metabolic syndrome components in healthy, non-athletic Muslim people aged over 15 years: a systematic review and meta-analysis", "summary": "Full-text methods section of a systematic review and meta-analysis (British Journal of Nutrition, 2019, PMID 31581955) examining how Ramadan diurnal intermittent fasting (RDIF) affects the five International Diabetes Federation metabolic syndrome (MetS) components in healthy, non-diabetic, non-athletic Muslims aged over 15. The page details PRISMA-guided database searches across nine databases (CINAHL, Cochrane, EBSCOhost, Google Scholar, ProQuest Medical, PubMed/MEDLINE, ScienceDirect, Scopus, Web of Science) from 1950 to March 2019, strict inclusion/exclusion rules (excluding diabetics, children, athletes, pregnant women, and post-Ramadan washout studies >1 month), and pre-post Hedges' g effect-size calculation with random-effects modelling in Comprehensive Meta-Analysis v2 and Stata 15. The authors pooled 85 studies with 4,326 participants, approximately 64% male, median age 31.5 years (range 15-80), across 23 countries, with mean daily fasting length of 828 minutes (SD 90; range 668-1038). Leave-one-out sensitivity analyses, meta-regression, country subgroup analyses, and funnel-plot/trim-and-fill publication bias checks were all performed. This page documents the authoritative methodology but does not contain headline effect-size numbers.", "perspectives": ["Regional", "Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["85 studies pooled with 4,326 total participants, conducted in 23 countries between 1982 and 2019", "Approximately 64% male participants; median age 31.5 years (range 15-80)", "Mean Ramadan fasting duration 828 minutes/day (SD 90; range 668-1038 minutes)", "PRISMA-compliant search 1950-March 2019 across 9 databases; Hedges' g effect size with random-effects model", "Pre-post design only; diabetics, athletes, children <18, pregnant/lactating women explicitly excluded"], "knowledge_contribution": "Establishes Ramadan as the largest real-world model of time-restricted diurnal intermittent fasting ever meta-analysed in a general healthy Muslim population, substantially expanding the Regional perspective beyond typical North American/European RCT cohorts. Documents that 85 pooled studies across 23 countries all used uncontrolled pre-post designs in free-living communities - the closest existing approximation to a population-scale natural experiment in IF - but none selected for metabolic syndrome, so translation to MetS adults requires cautious inference."}, {"url": "https://www.nmcd-journal.com/article/S0939-4753(21)00223-4/abstract", "title": "Does four-week consecutive, dawn-to-sunset intermittent fasting during Ramadan affect cardiometabolic risk factors in healthy adults? A systematic review, meta-analysis, and meta-regression", "summary": "Abstract-level content from Nutrition, Metabolism and Cardiovascular Diseases (2021, PMID 34167865) reporting a parallel systematic review and meta-analysis of Ramadan dawn-to-sunset intermittent fasting (RDIF) on cardiometabolic risk factors not covered in the MetS-components paper: total cholesterol (TC), triglycerides (TG), HDL-C, LDL-C, VLDL-C, diastolic blood pressure (DBP), and heart rate (HR). Ten databases searched from 1950 to November 2020. Pooled 91 studies with 4,431 adults aged 18-85 years, conducted in 23 countries across four continents between 1982 and 2020. Results show small but directionally favourable changes: HDL-C Hedges' g = 0.138 (95% CI 0.051, 0.224) and LDL-C g = -0.115 (95% CI -0.197, -0.034) reached significance; TC (g=-0.092), TG (g=-0.127), VLDL-C (g=-0.252), DBP (g=-0.255), and HR (g=-0.082) did not. Meta-regression identified age as a moderator of HDL-C and VLDL-C change, male sex a moderator of LDL-C change, and fasting duration the strongest moderator of HDL-C change (P=0.001). The abstract is substantive - not a paywall shell.", "perspectives": ["Regional", "Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["91 studies pooled with 4,431 adults (18-85 years), 23 countries, 1982-2020", "HDL-C Hedges' g = 0.138 (95% CI 0.051, 0.224) - small significant increase", "LDL-C Hedges' g = -0.115 (95% CI -0.197, -0.034) - small significant decrease", "Non-significant changes: TC g=-0.092, TG g=-0.127, VLDL-C g=-0.252, DBP g=-0.255, HR g=-0.082", "Fasting duration significant moderator of HDL-C (P=0.001); age moderator of HDL-C (P=0.02) and VLDL-C (P=0.01)"], "knowledge_contribution": "Extends the Regional perspective by quantifying additional lipid-profile and DBP effects of Ramadan fasting beyond the five canonical IDF MetS components, again in a genuinely population-scale sample from 23 countries. Shows that even in healthy adults RDIF produces only small CMRF shifts, with HDL-C rise being the most robust effect - this tempers any claim that Ramadan is a potent metabolic intervention and directly calibrates expectations when generalising IF to adults with metabolic syndrome."}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7468808/", "title": "Effects of Time-Restricted Feeding during Ramadan on Dietary ...", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT: paywalled/shell", "perspectives": ["Regional"], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": "Page returned only a PMC browser-verification redirect stub (under 250 characters, no article text). Adds nothing to Regional perspective coverage and cannot be cited. Re-fetch with a browser-headed client or direct PMID lookup would be required to extract actual findings."}, {"url": "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/335927480_Impact_of_Ramadan_Diurnal_Intermittent_Fasting_on_Metabolic_Syndrome_Components_in_Healthy_Non-Athletic_Muslim_People_Aged_Over_15_Years_A_Systematic_Review_and_Meta-Analysis", "title": "(PDF) Impact of Ramadan Diurnal Intermittent Fasting on Metabolic Syndrome Components in Healthy Non-Athletic Muslim People Aged Over 15 Years: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT: paywalled/shell", "perspectives": ["Regional"], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": "Page returned only a ResearchGate Cloudflare security-check CAPTCHA page (under 600 characters, no article text). This is the same underlying 2019 systematic review as the Cambridge.org and PubMed entries, so the lost access does not create a unique knowledge gap - the same meta-analytic findings are fully captured from the PubMed abstract. Adds nothing new to Regional coverage."}, {"url": "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0939475324000735", "title": "The effect of observing religious or faith-based fasting on cardiometabolic risk factors in healthy adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis", "summary": "Full-text introduction and methods of a 2024 Nutrition, Metabolism and Cardiovascular Diseases systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42022352197, PRISMA-compliant) examining cardiometabolic effects of religious/faith-based fasting in healthy adults compared with matched non-fasting controls. Unlike the Cambridge and NMCD 2021 reviews which pooled uncontrolled pre-post designs, this review explicitly required a non-fasting comparator group and excluded interventional studies on ethical grounds. Searches ran across PubMed, Embase, CINAHL, APA PsycInfo, and MEDLINE from inception to March 2023 (updated January 2024). After screening 3,430 records, 14 observational studies from 1998-2021 met inclusion criteria (10 contributed quantitative meta-analysis data). Included studies encompassed 320 Ramadan fasters with 224 non-fasting controls, and 435 Orthodox Christian fasters with 437 non-fasting controls. Outcomes measured: systolic/diastolic BP, BMI, triglycerides, total cholesterol, LDL-C, HDL-C, and fasting plasma glucose. Context notes 85% of the world population is religious, with ~2 billion Muslims practicing Ramadan and Orthodox Christians fasting up to 180-200 days/year in a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern. Effect-size results lie beyond the fetched window.", "perspectives": ["Regional", "Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["14 observational studies (1998-2021) included; 10 contributed meta-analysis quantitative data", "Ramadan arm: 320 fasting adults vs. 224 matched non-fasting controls", "Orthodox Christian arm: 435 fasting adults vs. 437 matched non-fasting controls", "PROSPERO-registered (CRD42022352197); PRISMA-compliant; required non-fasting comparator; interventional studies excluded", "Orthodox Christian fasting spans ~180-200 days/year with Mediterranean-style dietary restriction"], "knowledge_contribution": "Unique contribution to Regional and Methodological perspectives: the only captured review that uses matched non-fasting controls rather than pre-post designs, directly addressing the selection-bias limitation of the Cambridge/NMCD pooled analyses. Extends Regional coverage beyond Muslim-majority countries to include Orthodox Christian populations (Greek, Eastern, Coptic) and contrasts time-restricted vs. dietary-restricted religious fasting - directly relevant to whether IF effects generalise across faith-based real-world cohorts, though the sample (320 Ramadan + 435 Orthodox) is far smaller than the 4,326-participant pre-post meta-analyses."}, {"url": "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31581955/", "title": "Impact of Ramadan diurnal intermittent fasting on the metabolic syndrome components in healthy, non-athletic Muslim people aged over 15 years: a systematic review and meta-analysis", "summary": "Full abstract (PMID 31581955; DOI 10.1017/S000711451900254X; British Journal of Nutrition 2019) of the same Cambridge.org systematic review. This page is NOT a stub - it contains the complete headline effect-size results and is the most citation-dense page in the batch. Eighty-five studies with 4,326 participants across 23 countries (1982-2019) were pooled using Hedges' g with random-effects modelling. RDIF produced small but directionally favourable changes in all five IDF metabolic syndrome components: waist circumference reduction (K=24, N=1557, g=-0.312, 95% CI -0.387 to -0.236), systolic blood pressure reduction (K=22, N=1172, g=-0.239, 95% CI -0.372 to -0.106), triglyceride reduction (K=63, N=2862, g=-0.088, 95% CI -0.171 to -0.004), a fasting glucose trend (K=51, N=2318, g=-0.101, 95% CI -0.260 to 0.004, crosses null), and a HDL-cholesterol increase (K=57, N=2771, g=0.150, 95% CI 0.064 to 0.236). The authors conclude RDIF yields small improvements in all five MetS components in healthy people.", "perspectives": ["Regional", "Scientific", "Methodological", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["Waist circumference: K=24, N=1557, Hedges' g = -0.312 (95% CI -0.387, -0.236) - largest effect", "Systolic BP: K=22, N=1172, Hedges' g = -0.239 (95% CI -0.372, -0.106)", "HDL-cholesterol: K=57, N=2771, Hedges' g = 0.150 (95% CI 0.064, 0.236) - significant increase", "Triglycerides: K=63, N=2862, Hedges' g = -0.088 (95% CI -0.171, -0.004) - small significant decrease", "Fasting glucose: K=51, N=2318, Hedges' g = -0.101 (95% CI -0.260, 0.004) - crosses null, not significant"], "knowledge_contribution": "Provides the definitive quantitative anchor for the Regional perspective: Ramadan IF applied at population scale across 23 countries yields only small-magnitude Hedges' g effects (0.09-0.31) on MetS components in healthy adults. The strongest effect is waist circumference reduction; fasting glucose change is not statistically significant. This is crucial context when generalising IF benefits to adults with established metabolic syndrome, because even in a 4,326-person real-world sample of healthy Muslims the effect magnitudes fall into the small Cohen's-d band, cautioning against claims of large metabolic benefit from 12-18 hour daily time-restricted eating alone."}]}
